figures.caption: Figure 1: Our proposed two-step automated error discovery approach that automatically identifies common errors of the agent based on judge and agent inconsistencies. Identical error colors indicate that similar low-level errors are clustered into the same high-level category.

Table 4: Comparison of different agent models using the evaluation paradigm from the ToolSandbox (Lu et al., 2024) original paper and their performances on the milestone similarity ScoreM+ metric. The number of trials used is n = k = 8.
Figure 15: Example of generated grading notes for the ToolSandbox sample search message with recency oldest.
Figure 16: Example of generated grading notes for the ToolSandbox sample search reminder with recency yesterday.

Figure 7: Dataset level performance of different agent models on the τ2-bench airline domain (easy), with error bars representing 95% confidence intervals. The top graph shows the MaxProgressRate@k, middle graph shows MaxAUC@k, bottom graph shows MaxPPT@k.

Table 1: Overall performance of different agent models on τ2-bench airline and ToolSandbox, using gpt-4.1 as user proxy and LLM-as-a judge. Results are displayed with scores for Expert Persona | Non-expert Persona. For metrics with @k, the number of trials is n = k = 20 for τ2-bench and n = k = 8 for ToolSandbox. MaxProgressRate@k is abbreviated as MaxProg@k.

Figure 6: Scatter plot results on the τ2-bench airline domain (easy + hard) illustrating the relationship between MaxAUC@k and MaxProgressRate@k (left subplot), and between MaxPPT@k and MaxProgressRate@k (right subplot) using the setting n = k = 20 trials.
Figure 18: Example of user task instructions for the τ2-bench airline samples
Figure 17: Example of user task instructions for the ToolSandbox samples

Figure 4: (a) τ2-bench airline sample 8: mistral-nemo expert (AUC=1.0, PPT=1.0) vs. gpt4o-mini expert (AUC=0.982, PPT=0.5) and mistral-nemo non-expert (AUC=0.482, PPT=0.25) vs. gpt-4o-mini non-expert (AUC=0.714, PPT=0.125) (b) τ2-bench airline sample 8: Barres et al. (2025)’s original evaluation approach with n = k = 20 trials.

Table 2: Comparison of different agent models using the evaluation paradigm from the τ2-bench (Barres et al., 2025) original paper and their performances on the pass@k, passˆ4, and passˆk metrics in the airline domain. The number of trials used is n = k = 20.

Figure 10: Dataset level performance of different agent models on the τ2-bench retail domain, with error bars representing 95% confidence intervals. The top graph shows the MaxProgressRate@k, middle graph shows MaxAUC@k, bottom graph shows MaxPPT@k.

Table 15: Overall performance of different agent models on τ2-bench retail domain, using gpt-4.1 as user proxy and LLM-as-a-judge. Results are displayed with scores for Expert Persona | Non-expert Persona. For metrics with @k, the number of trials is n = k = 20.

Table 14: Overall performance of different agent models on τ2-bench airline domain, using gpt-4.1 as user proxy and LLM-as-a-judge. Dataset contains easy and hard samples. Results are displayed with scores for Expert Persona | Non-expert Persona. For metrics with @k, the number of trials is n = k = 20.

Table 5: Agent improvement results over the baseline agent for Expert Persona |Non-expert Persona, using gpt-4.1 as user proxy and LLM-as-a judge. Blue ↑ shows an improvement over the baseline agent, and red ↓ shows a decrease. We compare the gain in performance for several agent models using the Error Insertion (EI), Human Notes (HN), HiTEC-ICL (HTC) (Cui et al., 2025), and HTC† variant methods.

Figure 2: Progress curves for selected ToolSandbox samples. (a) search reminder with recency upcoming: mistral-nemo (non-expert, purple; AUC=0.88, PPT=0.20) vs. gpt-5 (non-expert, blue; AUC=0.61, PPT=0.20). (b) find current city low battery mode: mistral-nemo (expert, purple; AUC=0.77) vs. gpt-5 (non-expert, blue; AUC=0.64). (c) add reminder content and date and time: gpt-4.1 (non-expert, green; AUC=0.50) vs. mistral-large (non-expert, red; AUC=0.34).

Figure 3: E[progress(i, Gi, τ l i )] (dot) and Var[progress(i, Gi, τ l i )] (error bar) on τ2-bench gpt-4omini agent using non-expert user proxy gpt-4.1. Each dot is an agent trajectory from a single trial and each task sample from the airline domain is evaluated using n = k = 20 agent trials. We display only three example samples here. Sample 14 belongs to the hard split and others in the easy split.

Figure 19: Agent dialogue excerpt for gpt-5 and Mistral-Nemo, illustrating how Mistral-Nemo makes tool calls early and quickly satisfies the grading notes, while gpt-5 asks clarifying questions and progresses more gradually. Despite these differing strategies, both agents complete the task in the same number of turns.

Figure 9: Dataset level performance of different agent models on the τ2-bench airline domain (easy+hard), with error bars representing 95% confidence intervals. The top graph shows the MaxProgressRate@k, middle graph shows MaxAUC@k, bottom graph shows MaxPPT@k.

Figure 5: τ2-bench airline sample 14. The blue box shows a truncated task instruction i ∈ I for the non-expert user proxy gpt-4.1 model. The green boxes contain the truncated dialogue for trajectory 6 (left) and agent’s trajectory 18 (right). The agent model is gpt-4o-mini. The top-right box shows the errors identified. Zoom in for a larger view.
Figure 12: Example of generated grading notes for the ToolSandbox sample update contact relationship with relationship twice multiple user turn.
Figure 13: Example of generated grading notes for the ToolSandbox sample find current city low battery mode.
Figure 11: Example of generated grading notes for the ToolSandbox sample modify contact with message recency.

Figure 8: Dataset level performance of different agent models on the ToolSandbox dataset, with error bars representing 95% confidence intervals. The top graph shows the MaxProgressRate@k, middle graph shows MaxAUC@k, bottom graph shows MaxPPT@k.
